critters.name: White-tailed Deer
critters.summary: Early-summer edge walkers now grazing hard while does get extra secretive and bucks start showing soft velvet growth.
critters.fact: White-tailed deer use their tail to signal danger by flashing its white underside as a warning.
critters.activeFact: Right now, deer may be active near clover, trail edges, and openings, but does can vanish into cover fast if fawns are tucked nearby.

critters.name: Black Bear

critters.fact: Black bears have an excellent sense of smell, even better than bloodhounds.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bear movement can increase near wet bottoms, berry patches forming up, rotting logs, and quiet feeding pockets with easy snacks.

critters.name: Coyote
critters.summary: Lean trail-route operator now hunting a busier landscape full of rabbits, rodents, nests, and young spring wildlife.
critters.fact: Coyotes can adapt to various environments, from deserts to cities, showing impressive survival skills.
critters.activeFact: Right now, coyotes may be working edges, two-tracks, and open lanes at dawn or dusk while hauling groceries back toward den areas.

critters.name: Red Fox
critters.summary: Rust-colored mouse sniper now balancing sharp hunting with the nonstop grocery run of kit season.
critters.fact: Red foxes have vertically slit pupils, helping them see better in low light while hunting.
critters.activeFact: Right now, foxes may be seen crossing openings with prey or pausing in grass where rodents are moving through thicker green cover.

critters.name: Gray Fox
critters.summary: Brush-country ghost staying tucked in the green-up while quietly running its own family-and-food operation.
critters.fact: Gray foxes are unique canids known for their ability to climb trees skillfully.
critters.activeFact: Right now, gray fox activity is most likely along thick edges, brushy travel lanes, and shadowed timber where visibility is getting tighter.

critters.name: Bobcat
critters.summary: Silent timber stalker now using thicker cover and busier prey movement like the woods built it a hunting blind.
critters.fact: Bobcats can leap up to 10 feet high to catch prey or escape danger swiftly.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bobcats may be working brush lines, trail crossings, and transition zones where young animals and small prey are moving more.

critters.name: Porcupine
critters.summary: Slow-motion tree tank now enjoying greener choices but still acting like bark is part of a balanced breakfast.
critters.fact: Porcupines have around 30,000 sharp quills for self-defense, which detach easily when threatened.
critters.activeFact: Right now, porcupines may be feeding in leafy trees, quiet edges, or woody patches where fresh growth is easy to reach.

critters.name: Beaver
critters.summary: Creek engineer still on duty, patching water problems like the wetlands keep submitting maintenance tickets.
critters.fact: Beavers build dams to create ponds, providing protection from predators and access to food during winter.
critters.activeFact: Right now, fresh sticks, muddy slides, peeled branches, and newly packed dam spots are all signs the night crew has been busy.

critters.name: Muskrat
critters.summary: Pond commuter fully into warm-season shoreline life, popping around cattails like a tiny wetland boat captain.
critters.fact: Muskrats build dome-shaped lodges from plants, providing shelter and protection in wetlands.
critters.activeFact: Right now, muskrats may be swimming pond edges, feeding on green aquatic growth, or slipping through cattails at dawn and dusk.

critters.name: Mink
critters.summary: Shoreline menace now patrolling frog-and-fish country with the confidence of something built for trouble.
critters.fact: Minks use sharp teeth and strong jaws to catch fish, their favorite meal in the wild.
critters.activeFact: Right now, mink activity may show along muddy banks, creek edges, pond margins, and narrow routes where prey is stacked close to water.

critters.name: Fisher
critters.summary: Deep-woods prowler moving through thicker green timber like it prefers the forest with the curtains closed.
critters.fact: Fishers are skilled climbers and can hunt porcupines by flipping them over to avoid quills.
critters.activeFact: Right now, fishers may be mostly unseen in heavier woods, with trail cameras or tracks giving the best clue they passed through.

critters.name: River Otter
critters.summary: Water-slide troublemaker now working warmer creeks and ponds where fish, frogs, and mud all say yes.
critters.fact: River otters can hold their breath underwater for up to eight minutes while hunting for food.
critters.activeFact: Right now, otters may be cruising channels, using muddy slides, and stirring up pond or creek edges where aquatic life is active.

critters.name: Eastern Gray Squirrel
critters.summary: Treetop chaos clerk now juggling leafy cover, young squirrels, feeder raids, and branch-to-branch nonsense.
critters.fact: Eastern Gray Squirrels can rotate their hind feet 180 degrees to climb down trees headfirst.
critters.activeFact: Right now, gray squirrels are busy in leafy trees, around feeders, and along branches where young squirrels may start adding extra circus energy.

critters.name: American Red Squirrel
critters.summary: Tiny red alarm siren still defending the woods like every pine cone is protected by federal law.
critters.fact: American Red Squirrels fiercely defend their territory and food caches year-round to survive harsh winters.
critters.activeFact: Right now, red squirrels may be extra loud around conifers, brush piles, and feeding areas where they refuse to share the stage.

critters.name: Chipmunk
critters.summary: Pocket rocket fully back in summer traffic, stuffing cheeks and treating every log like a launch ramp.
critters.fact: Chipmunks can store food in their cheek pouches to carry back to their burrows quickly.
critters.activeFact: Right now, chipmunks are darting across trails, logs, and rock edges while collecting food and ducking into burrow entrances.

critters.name: Snowshoe Hare

critters.fact: Snowshoe hares change fur color seasonally for camouflage, white in winter and brown in summer.

critters.name: Eastern Cottontail
critters.summary: Brush-edge hopper now feeding through green abundance while keeping one foot near the panic button.
critters.fact: Eastern Cottontails have nearly 360-degree vision to spot predators quickly while staying alert and safe.
critters.activeFact: Right now, cottontails may be feeding along lawns, trail edges, clover patches, and brush piles, especially near dawn and dusk.

critters.name: Raccoon

critters.fact: Raccoons have highly sensitive front paws, enabling them to open jars and solve complex puzzles.
critters.activeFact: Right now, raccoons may be working pond edges, creeks, feeders, campsites, and trash zones with criminal little hand energy.

critters.name: Opossum

critters.fact: Opossums have a remarkable immune system that makes them resistant to many snake venoms and diseases.
critters.activeFact: Right now, opossums may be active around sheds, brush, compost areas, road edges, and camp margins during warmer nights.

critters.name: Striped Skunk
critters.summary: Striped lawn inspector now rooting through soft ground like every grub owes back rent.
critters.fact: Striped skunks can spray a foul-smelling liquid up to 10 feet to deter predators.
critters.activeFact: Right now, skunks may be nosing through lawns, trail edges, damp soil, and field margins where bug life is picking up.

critters.name: Flying Squirrel

critters.fact: Flying squirrels glide using a special membrane called a patagium stretched between their limbs.
critters.activeFact: Right now, nighttime cameras, feeders, cavities, or nest boxes may catch them moving quietly through leafy tree lanes.

critters.fact: Little Brown Bats use echolocation to catch insects mid-air with incredible precision during nighttime flights.
critters.activeFact: Right now, mild nights may bring feeding loops over ponds, clearings, trails, and tree lines where mosquitoes and small flies are moving.

critters.name: Big Brown Bat

critters.fact: Big Brown Bats use echolocation to catch insects mid-flight, helping control pest populations naturally.
critters.activeFact: Right now, they may appear near buildings, lights, woodland openings, and pond edges when warmer evenings bring bugs up.

critters.name: Barred Owl
critters.summary: Deep-woods night boss still calling through leafy timber while young mouths may be keeping the schedule busy.
critters.fact: Barred Owls have distinctive hoots that sound like "Who cooks for you? Who cooks for you all?"

critters.fact: Great Horned Owls have powerful talons that can exert over 28 pounds of pressure, perfect for hunting prey.

critters.name: Blue Jay
critters.summary: Blue feathered security department now defending nest space with a volume knob stuck on rude.
critters.fact: Blue Jays can mimic hawk calls to scare away other birds from their territory.
critters.activeFact: Right now, blue jays may be loud, aggressive, and extremely interested in anything moving near their trees or feeder routes.

critters.name: Northern Cardinal
critters.summary: Red morning announcer still posted in the brush, singing like the day does not start without approval.

critters.activeFact: Right now, cardinals are active around shrubs, feeders, thickets, and woodland edges where nesting pairs stay busy.

critters.name: Black-capped Chickadee

critters.fact: Black-capped Chickadees can remember hundreds of hiding spots for their food throughout the seasons.

critters.name: Tufted Titmouse
critters.summary: Gray little loudspeaker now working leafy branches with nest-season confidence and unnecessary commentary.
critters.fact: Tufted Titmice can remember thousands of cache locations to find hidden food later.

critters.name: White-breasted Nuthatch
critters.summary: Upside-down bark runner still treating tree trunks like sidewalks while nest season keeps it busy.

critters.name: Downy Woodpecker
critters.summary: Small bark drummer now working nest duty and bug patrol with tiny hammer precision.
critters.fact: Downy Woodpeckers drum on trees to communicate and establish territory.

critters.name: Hairy Woodpecker
critters.summary: Bigger timber hammer still sounding off from mature trees while the bug hunt gets serious.
critters.fact: Hairy Woodpeckers use their long tongues to extract insects from deep tree bark crevices.

critters.fact: Red-bellied Woodpeckers drum on trees to communicate and establish territory, not just to find food.

critters.fact: Mourning Doves can fly up to 55 miles per hour to escape predators.
critters.activeFact: Right now, paired doves may be cooing, carrying twigs, sitting nests, or making questionable construction choices in plain sight.

critters.summary: Brightening seed specialist now looking more summer-ready but still waiting for peak nesting timing.
critters.fact: American Goldfinches molt twice a year, changing from bright yellow in summer to olive brown in winter.

critters.summary: Cool-weather ground feeder mostly fading from the main stage as summer birds take over the playlist.
critters.fact: Dark-eyed Juncos change their song patterns daily to communicate with neighbors during breeding season.

critters.name: House Finch
critters.summary: Social little singer now fully in neighborhood-radio mode, broadcasting from feeders and brush tops.
critters.fact: House Finches are known for their cheerful, warbling songs that vary regionally across North America.
critters.activeFact: Right now, house finches may be paired, nesting, singing, and making steady feeder visits with summer confidence.

critters.summary: Rosy woodland singer still possible in northern cover, drifting through like it enjoys being hard to schedule.
critters.fact: Male Purple Finches have a raspberry-red crown, face, and chest, making them vibrant and easily recognizable.
critters.activeFact: Right now, purple finches may show up around forest edges and feeders, but appearances can still feel brief and unpredictable.

critters.name: Wild Turkey
critters.summary: Strut-season drama crew now shifting toward hens nesting while gobblers still act like show business is alive.
critters.fact: Wild turkeys can fly up to 55 miles per hour for short distances to escape predators.
critters.activeFact: Right now, hens may be secretive in cover, while toms can still appear in openings acting like the season has one encore left.

critters.name: Ruffed Grouse

critters.fact: Ruffed Grouse create drumming sounds by rapidly beating their wings to establish territory and attract mates.

critters.name: Sandhill Crane
critters.summary: Tall marsh dinosaur now deep into nesting or colt-raising season with maximum voice and minimum subtlety.
critters.fact: Sandhill Cranes communicate using loud, trumpeting calls that can be heard over long distances during migration.
critters.activeFact: Right now, cranes may be guarding wetland territories, feeding in open areas, or moving with young nearby if nesting succeeded.

critters.name: American Crow
critters.summary: Black-feathered property auditor now running nest-season surveillance on everything suspicious, edible, or shiny.
critters.fact: American Crows use tools like sticks to extract insects from tree bark.
critters.activeFact: Right now, crows may be loud, watchful, and quick to mob hawks, owls, or anything they decide is bad news.

critters.name: Common Raven
critters.summary: Big woodland trickster cruising over green timber like it knows the punchline before anyone else hears the joke.

critters.name: Red-tailed Hawk
critters.summary: Airborne landlord now tied tight to nest territory while every careless field mouse is living dangerously.
critters.fact: Red-tailed Hawks can spot prey from over a mile away using their incredible vision.

critters.fact: Cooper’s Hawks are skilled fliers, weaving through dense trees to catch birds mid-air with sharp agility.
critters.activeFact: Right now, quick low flights through trees may mean a Cooper’s hawk is hunting or patrolling near an active nest.

critters.name: Mallard Duck
critters.summary: Classic pond duck now moving through early-summer water while hens keep nesting and brood plans quiet.
critters.fact: Mallard ducks can sleep with one eye open to stay alert for predators.
critters.activeFact: Right now, mallards may be loafing, feeding, nesting, or leading ducklings through shallow pond edges and flooded vegetation.

critters.name: Wood Duck
critters.summary: Flashy backwater duck now slipping through green pockets where ducklings may already be tiny chaos boats.
critters.fact: Wood Ducks nest in tree cavities, often using abandoned woodpecker holes near water.
critters.activeFact: Right now, wood ducks may be hiding in sheltered pond edges, flooded timber, and quiet bends where young broods stay protected.

critters.name: Canada Goose
critters.summary: Pond security staff now upgraded to gosling protection mode, which somehow made them even less reasonable.
critters.fact: Canada Geese can fly over 1,500 miles nonstop during migration.

critters.name: Great Blue Heron

critters.fact: Painted Turtles absorb UV light to help produce vitamin D for healthy shell growth.

critters.name: Bluegill

critters.fact: Largemouth bass can expand their jaws to swallow prey half their size whole.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bass may be holding tight to beds, shallow cover, or nearby drop-offs depending on water temperature and spawning stage.

critters.name: Yellow Perch
critters.summary: Striped school fish now past the cold-water rush and sliding into warmer feeding lanes.
critters.fact: Yellow Perch have distinct dark vertical stripes, which help them blend into underwater vegetation.

critters.name: Northern Pike
critters.summary: Toothy green missile now past early spawning and back to ambush duty in weedy lanes.
critters.fact: Northern Pike have sharp teeth and use ambush tactics to catch prey with sudden bursts of speed.

critters.name: Spring Peeper

critters.name: Green Frog

critters.fact: Green frogs communicate by making a distinctive plucking sound, often heard near ponds and marshes.

critters.fact: Water snakes can hold their breath underwater for up to 30 minutes while hunting or hiding from predators.
critters.activeFact: Right now, water snakes may be basking near pond edges, sliding into shallows, or holding close to sunny banks.

critters.name: Monarch Butterfly

critters.fact: Monarch butterflies migrate up to 3,000 miles, traveling from North America to central Mexico for winter.

critters.summary: Big-winged summer showoff now entering the chat as warm afternoons and flowers finally cooperate.

critters.fact: Dragonflies can fly up to 30 miles per hour and are skilled hunters of mosquitoes and other insects.

critters.fact: Damselflies can fold their wings along their body, unlike dragonflies that keep wings spread out.
critters.activeFact: Right now, damselflies may begin hovering near shoreline plants, reeds, and calm water on warm still days.

critters.name: Firefly

critters.fact: Fireflies use bioluminescence to attract mates and communicate with unique light patterns in the dark.

critters.name: Honeybee

critters.fact: Honeybees communicate by performing a "waggle dance" to show food locations to hive mates.

critters.name: Bumblebee
critters.summary: Fuzzy pollinator crew now expanding beyond queen scouts as early colonies start getting real.
critters.fact: Bumblebees can regulate their body temperature by vibrating their flight muscles to stay warm.

critters.name: Grasshopper

critters.fact: Grasshoppers can jump up to 20 times their body length using powerful hind legs.
